sentences.text: Task 1. Listen to the text and for each question mark the correct answer.
A, B, C, or D.
You now have 40 seconds to look through the task.
You will then hear the recording twice.
Good morning, dear students, and welcome to my lecture on British Cultural Studies.
This is part of a one-semester course leading to an exam in January.
We will meet once a week, and you will also meet with your tutor, Miss Helen Brown,
every two weeks to discuss practical matters related to your studies.
Today's lecture is about Oxford University.
Oxford University is located in the city of Oxford, about 90 kilometers from London.
Here are some key points about Oxford.
The university began operating around 1096, which makes it the oldest university in the English-speaking world,
and the second oldest in the world after the University of Bologna in Italy.
Two other old British universities are the University of Cambridge, founded in 1209,
and the University of St. Andrews, founded in 1413.
All these universities have continuously provided education,
which means that the university is now a university of its own.
Throughout the centuries, they have never stopped teaching.
Oxford University expanded rapidly, starting in 1167.
The reason was that King Henry II banned English students from attending the University of Paris.
Many people think that Oxford University is just one building, which is not true.
It actually consists of 39 autonomous colleges, each with its own administration and activities.
The University of Oxford offers more than 330 courses across various disciplines,
including science, arts, humanities, and social sciences.
The oldest faculties at Oxford are theology, law, and medicine.
The most recent department, the Department of Biology, was established in 2022,
combining the former departments of zoology and plant sciences.
One distinctive feature of Oxford is its tutorial system.
In tutorials, students meet in small groups with a tutor to discuss their work and receive personalized guidance in their field of study.
Oxford's teaching style is centered around these conversations typically between two or three students and their tutor.
Tutorials provide a unique opportunity for individualized feedback and valuable academic recommendations.
Getting into Oxford is very competitive.
You need excellent grades, and you must be prepared to take the courses.
You need excellent grades, and you must be prepared to take the courses.
You need excellent grades, and you must be prepared to take the courses.
You need excellent grades, and you must perform well in interviews and exams.
Additionally, you must submit a personal statement explaining why you want to study there.
Additionally, you must submit a personal statement explaining why you want to study there.
Each year, over 23,000 young people apply for 3,300 undergraduate places.
This means that Oxford receives around seven applications for each available place.
Now let's talk about the Bodleian Library.
The Bodleian Library in Oxford, founded in 1602 by Sir Thomas Bodley,
is one of the largest libraries in Europe.
It houses millions of books, maps, and manuscripts.
With over 13 million printed items, the Bodleian Library is the second largest library in Britain,
after the British Library.
Its collection includes items from around the world, such as a collection of books and documents from Georgia,
a small country in the South Caucasus.
A British diplomat, Sir Oliver Wardrop, and his sister Marjorie,
played a significant role in expanding the library.
He even have the same collection at the Bodleian Library,
which now consists of 1454 items,
most of which have been digitised.
Marjorie Wardrop also made the first English prose translation of the Georgian epic poem
The Knight in the Panther's Skin by Shota Roustavelli,
which was first published in London in 1912.
Oxford University is associated with many gifted individuals,
including 28 British Prime Minister's,
Dr. John Burton,
from England,
and the best British author of the New York Times.
These are a wide range ofavia and modern literature,
which were published from 1817 to 1915.
30 international leaders, 55 Nobel Prize winners, and around 150 Olympic medalists.
